title: I've Traded Forex For 10 Years. What Actually Matters?

full_text: I've been trading Forex for a decade. And in this video, I'm going to break down three things that I believe are most important, the things that really, really matter. So, the first thing right here is don't try to predict which way the market's going. Instead, use technical analysis, and in my case, it's just support and resistance as a way to control risk. So a lot of people whenever they're looking at the charts, they're looking at the charts in such a way where they're trying to predict which way price is going to go. You know, price is in an uptrend. It's making higher highs and higher lows. So we have to we have to wait for it to pull back because we predict it's going to go up. So you're trying to optimize for that outcome. Whereas in reality, nobody can predict which way price will go even a high percentage of the time. Most of the people that I personally know that are profitable in my peer group of profitable tra prof profitable traders, they do not get things right more than like 70% of the time. And most of them have win rates closer to 50 and 60%. And the only reason they're profitable is because they control risk, which is actually the next thing we're going to get into. But for this first part right here, instead of predicting the charts and trying to speculate on which way price will go, instead you can use technical analysis as just a way to control risk. So let me show you both of the frameworks through it. So the first framework in terms of trying to predict where price is going to go is saying okay price is here at the resistance zone and you're trying to make the assumption and trying to make let's even say the assertion that price might bounce away from this little resistance zone and go towards the downside. And if you approach things that way there's nothing wrong with that. But the thing is is it's going to make it a lot more difficult for you to remain consistent because you're getting in one-sided. You're thinking okay I'm optimizing for this outcome. I'm expecting price to go down. If it goes against you, you still have to have a plan of what you're going to do. Which is why I believe that practically it's more it's more important to just focus on structuring risk. So instead of using support resistance as a way to predict which way price will go, here's a way that I do it. I look at charts and I say support resistance is not great for predicting or speculating price, but it's a great way to control risk. Technical analysis and support resistance, there's zero predictive power in my 10 years of experience. They just give you a great way of controlling risk. Why? Because if price gets into an area, let's say a super clear support resistance zone on the daily, weekly charts, you can just enter a sell when price gets into a resistance zone. And I only enter trades on zones that are very, very clear and obvious. So things like this, this daily zone here, prices bounced at this zone very obviously, very clearly, and very cleanly, multiple times in the past. And now it's bouncing here as resistance. It's just an area where price has bounced multiple times. And so it looks nice and clean and nice and clear. And so when price gets into these zones, I can just enter a cell. I'll put my stop above and put my target below. And I'm using support resistance as a way to control risk instead of trying to predict which way price is going to go. So that's the first thing here. Don't use technical analysis to try to predict and speculate where price will go. Instead, just use it as a way to structure risk.
